tours.title: The theological foundations of nonviolence
tours.subtitle: How the Black church framed the discipline of unearned suffering.
tours.body:
Nonviolence as it was taught in the King-circle SCLC and the Nashville workshops was not a tactical compromise. It was a theological argument: that unearned suffering is redemptive, that the agape love at the center of the Christian gospel is also a method of social pressure, and that the Black church was the institution capable of organizing both. The voices in this tour were trained in seminaries (Boston University, Morehouse, Colgate Rochester, Howard School of Religion), pastored congregations, and ran the citizenship and direct-action programs that translated theology into mass meetings, marches, and jail vigils.

The path moves from architects (Walker, Lowery, the Abernathy family) to practitioners (Vivian, Sherrod, Anderson) to the trainer who scaled the doctrine across the South (Cotton). It closes on a Seattle pastor and a Mississippi minister whose accounts show how the SCLC framework traveled outside the canonical Birmingham-Selma corridor.

path.note: SCLC chief of staff and architect of Project C, the Birmingham strategic plan, who transcribed the Letter from Birmingham Jail from King's manuscript fragments. Frames nonviolence as engineered campaign discipline grounded in Baptist preaching.
SCLC co-founder and Methodist minister who articulates nonviolence in explicitly theological terms — agape, redemptive suffering, and the church as movement infrastructure.
Juanita and Donzaleigh Abernathy on Ralph D. Abernathy as Montgomery bus-boycott co-organizer and Poor People's Campaign successor — the pastoral partnership behind the public King.

Nashville nonviolence-workshop graduate under James Lawson and SCLC executive staff whose 1947 Peoria sit-in predates Greensboro by thirteen years. His confrontation with Jim Clark on the Selma courthouse steps shows nonviolent discipline under direct physical threat.

SNCC's first field secretary, ordained Baptist minister, who built the Albany Movement and the Southwest Georgia Project around mass meetings at Mt. Olive Baptist and the rural Black church network across Baker, Terrell, and Mitchell counties.

First president of the Albany Movement, the physician who brought King and SCLC into the 1961-62 campaign. His account joins church-based organizing to the professional Black middle class through the Criterion Club.

Director of SCLC's Citizenship Education Program (1960-1972), the institutional pipeline that trained 6,000 grassroots organizers at Dorchester Academy in collaboration with Septima Clark and Highlander.

Shreveport SCLC staff minister beaten unconscious inside Little Union Baptist Church during a memorial service for the Sixteenth Street Baptist bombing victims. Long pastorate at Mount Canaan Baptist extended the SCLC model into low-income housing and literacy programs.

Mount Zion Baptist Church (Seattle) pastor and Morehouse-Colgate Rochester contemporary of King, who hosted King's 1961 Seattle visit. Shows the King-circle theology operating outside the Southern corridor.

tours.closing: The doctrine was a discipline, not a feeling — what happens when the discipline meets a sheriff's club is what the testimony records.

tours.title: Mississippi voter registration, 1962-65
tours.subtitle: SNCC field organizing in the Delta and Forrest County.
tours.body:
Between Bob Moses's 1961 entry into McComb and the 1965 Voting Rights Act, SNCC field secretaries built a voter-registration apparatus in the Mississippi counties where federal protection did not reach. The work was conducted out of freedom houses that were firebombed, mass meetings at Black-owned country churches, and the homes of farmers and beauticians who absorbed the consequences after the SNCC workers moved on. Two registrar offices anchor the geography: Sunflower County under Cecil Campbell in Indianola, and Forrest County under Theron Lynd in Hattiesburg, both subjects of federal voting-rights suits.

This path begins with the SNCC field staff who arrived from Howard NAG, Tougaloo, and Pine Bluff, then moves to the local Mississippians who hosted them — the Greenes in Greenwood, the Magees and Dahmers in Hattiesburg, Bruce in Holmes County — and closes on the MFDP delegate apparatus that took the work to Atlantic City in 1964.

path.note: Sunflower County SNCC field secretary who drove Fannie Lou Hamer to her first registration attempt at Indianola in August 1962 and was in Greenwood the night Carmichael declared Black Power from a flatbed truck in 1966.
Baltimore CIG organizer dispatched to McComb in 1961 with Bob Moses — among the first SNCC field secretaries to combine voter registration with direct action in Pike County.

Greenwood native whose Leflore County family hosted Sam Block, Willie Peacock, and Carmichael during the 1962-63 drive. Her McComb freedom house was bombed days after the staff vote to bring in white volunteers.
MFDP chairman from Pass Christian; central figure in the Winona arrest with Fannie Lou Hamer and Annelle Ponder in June 1963, and in the Atlantic City delegate-challenge architecture.
Itta Bena SNCC worker who shared the Winona jail cell with Hamer through the night of the beating, then continued voter-registration work across the Delta with Guyot, Hollis Watkins, and Worth Long.

Birmingham-born SNCC field secretary who organized in Alabama and worked the Selma campaign alongside CORE, SCLC, and NAACP. Her testimony names the line between self-defense and overt violence in SNCC's later Black Power period.

Hattiesburg NAACP member who applied repeatedly to register under Theron Lynd, stood night watch at the Dahmer home after the firebombing, and testifies to the Forrest County clandestine self-defense network 'the deep guns.'

Widow of Vernon Dahmer Sr., the Forrest County NAACP president murdered by the White Knights of the KKK in January 1966. Her household sheltered Hollis Watkins, Curtis Hayes, and the Ladner sisters during Freedom Summer.

Hattiesburg MFDP executive secretary and named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the redistricting suit that produced Mississippi's first Black congressional district. The Atlantic City convention pipeline from a beauty shop.

Durant movement leader and Holmes County MFDP chair whose testimony covers the Mileston Fourteen, the firebombing of Hartman Turnbow's house, and the integration of Durant schools under Alexander v. Holmes.

tours.closing: The voting-rights infrastructure was built by people who lived where the federal government did not — what kept them safe enough to keep working is its own story.

tours.title: Women shaping SNCC
tours.subtitle: The under-credited intellectual and organizational labor.
tours.body:
The standard SNCC narrative names Carmichael, Forman, Sellers, and Moses. The voices in this tour built the organization's communications office, its photographic documentation, its Freedom Schools, its Atlantic City credentials strategy, its Black Power position paper, and its grass-roots field projects in Albany, Greenwood, Selma, Laurel, and Lowndes County. Several were later co-editors of Hands on the Freedom Plow, the corrective oral-history volume that recovered women's roles in SNCC after decades of obscurity.

The path moves from the Atlanta WATS-line office (Zellner) outward to the field projects (Norman Noonan, Greene), the documentary record (Derby), the Freedom School and Black Power theorizing (Simmons), and the post-1965 dispersal as some moved to the Black Panthers (Cleaver) while others stayed inside SNCC's strategic core (Richardson, Varela). It closes with the Jackson high-school marchers whose 1963 fairgrounds detention prefigured every subsequent Mississippi confrontation.

path.note: SNCC communications-office staff who edited the WATS-line dispatches during Freedom Summer under James Forman; the daily editorial filter that shaped Northern press coverage of the Mississippi work.

SNCC Atlanta-office staff, later co-producer of the Eyes on the Prize documentary series and co-editor of Hands on the Freedom Plow. The institutional memory of women's SNCC labor was largely her project.
Tougaloo Black-history instructor and Freedom Summer COFO Jackson-office staff who co-founded the Free Southern Theater and documented the Mississippi work as a photographer for Southern Media, Inc.
SDS recruit who organized voter registration in Albany, Greenwood, and Selma 1963-1967, working under Sherrod and Sam Block. Co-editor of Hands on the Freedom Plow.

Spelman student who at 19 ran the Laurel, Mississippi Freedom Summer project with a 23-volunteer staff; co-author of the Atlanta Project position paper on Black Power; later AFSC researcher on COINTELPRO surveillance.
SNCC literacy-program staff and one of the organization's only Chicana field workers; later a documentary photographer of the Tierra Amarilla land-rights movement in northern New Mexico.
SNCC field worker in Atlanta and Alabama before joining the Black Panther Party as communications secretary in 1967. Marks the SNCC-to-BPP migration of women cadre after the Black Power turn.

Jackson SNCC and NAACP Youth Council marcher held at the 1963 fairgrounds livestock pens Medgar Evers called a concentration camp; later White Plains BPP organizer who went underground for four years.

path.entry_subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
path.note: Tougaloo students from Palmer's Crossing, Mississippi who organized in the Hattiesburg COFO project and the Atlantic City MFDP challenge. Joyce later wrote The Death of White Sociology.

tours.closing: The question is not whether women built SNCC — the testimony settles that — but why the corrective took fifty years to publish.

tours.title: The Black Power turn
tours.subtitle: Generational discontinuity from nonviolence to armed self-defense.
tours.body:
The Black Power turn was not a single 1966 announcement on a Greenwood flatbed truck. It was a multi-year argument inside SNCC about whether nonviolence was a tactic or a way of life, a Howard NAG debate about colonial analysis, a Lowndes County experiment with independent third-party politics, and a Bay Area decision by two community-college students to form an armed police-watch patrol. The voices in this tour participated in all of those threads — and several record the COINTELPRO infiltration, internal splits, and post-1973 disintegration that followed.

The path begins inside SNCC's pre-1966 strategic apparatus (Cox, Sellers, Hutchings), traces the Lowndes County independent-party model, then crosses to the Black Panther Party's founding cadre in Oakland (Howard), Seattle (the Dixon brothers), Chicago (McCarty), Los Angeles (Mtume), and the Oakland national headquarters (Alexander, Cleaver, Hopkins). It closes with the survey of post-disintegration reflection.

path.note: Howard NAG co-founder who helped draft the Lowndes County Freedom Organization's research-grounded independent-party strategy in 1965 — the Black Panther logo's origin point.

SNCC program secretary who worked with Carmichael on Lowndes County and recounts the late-1967 meeting where he and Carmichael discussed Black Power with Dr. King. Three months in prison after the 1968 Orangeburg massacre.
Cleveland-born SNCC organizer active through the Carmichael chairmanship transition and the Black United Front in Washington. Documents the simultaneous SDS-SNCC organizing of the late 1960s.

Founding member of the Black Panther Party recruited by Huey Newton and Bobby Seale at Merritt College in 1966. Ran circulation of The Black Panther; defended the BPP at the 1967 Stockholm war-crimes tribunal.

Co-founder and captain of the Seattle BPP chapter (1968), the first chapter outside California. Deployed to Oakland under Bobby Seale during the Cleaver-Seale split between the armed-guerrilla and community-program wings.

Co-founded the Seattle BPP with his brother Aaron. Recounts the breakfast and busing-to-prisons programs, COINTELPRO surveillance, and the 1971 Newton-Cleaver split that divided East and West Coast Panthers.

Chicago BPP education-cadre member under Fred Hampton, expelled from Saint Ignatius for organizing a Black student walkout. Documents the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Lords and Young Patriots before COINTELPRO ended Hampton's life.
At nineteen ran the Bunchy Carter People's Free Clinic in South Central LA; later directed the George Jackson Free Clinic in Berkeley and served as Minister of Finance under Elaine Brown.
Oakland national-headquarters BPP member from 1969, recruited out of Berkeley. Daughter of an interracial couple raised in Adams Boulevard Los Angeles; later UCSD Third College faculty.

Oakland BPP newspaper writer 1972-74 under David Graham Du Bois who covered Bobby Seale's mayoral campaign. Traces COINTELPRO's role in his Winston-Salem recruitment and the post-1973 internal disintegration.

tours.closing: Whether Black Power was a break with nonviolence or its logical extension is a question the testimony refuses to settle on the easy side.

tours.title: Highlander Folk School and its alumni
tours.subtitle: The Tennessee mountain training school that incubated movement leaders.
tours.body:
Highlander, founded by Myles Horton at Monteagle, Tennessee in 1932 and re-founded as the Highlander Research and Education Center after the state revoked its charter in 1961, ran the integrated workshops where the freedom songs entered the movement, where Septima Clark designed the Citizenship Schools that taught literacy to register voters, and where SNCC's founding conference was nourished. Tennessee state troopers raided the school in 1957 during a workshop attended by Martin Luther King Jr.; the legal harassment that followed has long been documented at the National Archives.

This path moves through the music staff that put the songs into circulation, the workshop trainees who carried them into the field, the SCLC Citizenship Education director who scaled the Highlander method to 6,000 organizers, and the longtime board president who kept the institution alive across the relocation. It closes with two children whose accounts place Highlander inside the Montgomery and Cambridge campaigns.

path.entry_subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
path.note: Highlander music director (Guy) and longtime program director (Candie) who taught the freedom songs at the April 1960 SNCC founding conference. Introduced 'We Shall Overcome,' 'Keep Your Eyes on the Prize,' and 'We'll Never Turn Back' into the movement repertoire.

Folk musician who learned 'We Shall Overcome' from Zilphia Horton at Highlander in 1946 and sang it at the school's 1957 anniversary alongside King, Abernathy, and Rosa Parks — the night Tennessee troopers raided.
Highlander board member from 1958 and longtime president during the 1960s when the school hosted SNCC and SCLC strategy sessions. World War II Omaha Beach veteran and Sewanee French professor.
SCLC Citizenship Education director who scaled the Highlander method through Dorchester Academy training, in direct collaboration with Septima Clark and Myles Horton.
Second chair of SNCC (1960-1963) who was at the Shaw University founding conference. Argued against folding SNCC into SCLC and helped develop the jail-no-bail strategy first tested at Rock Hill.

Montgomery Bus Boycott walker at age 11 and Rosa Parks-mentored NAACP Youth Council member; credits a Highlander workshop with the origin of the 'We Are Not Afraid' verse of We Shall Overcome, sung the night state troopers cut the lights.

Hattiesburg beautician trained at Highlander's Dorchester Center, who collected poll tax for her customers and taught citizenship classes — the Highlander method running in a Forrest County beauty shop.

tours.closing: An integrated mountain school in 1932 Tennessee was an institutional bet that took thirty years to mature — what made it durable is worth studying.

tours.title: Witnesses to the Emmett Till case
tours.subtitle: The 1955 lynching and the people who carried its memory forward.
tours.body:
Emmett Till's August 1955 murder in Money, Mississippi, his open-casket funeral in Chicago, and the Sumner courthouse acquittal of J.W. Milam and Roy Bryant were the through-line event that pushed dozens of subsequent movement participants into activism. Two of the witnesses to the kidnapping itself were Till's cousins — Wheeler Parker Jr., the last living witness, and Simeon Wright, who shared the bedroom Till was taken from. Both later wrote correctives to documentary accounts they considered inaccurate. The corpus also contains journalist coverage of the funeral and NAACP staff who organized the national mobilization around the case.

This tour is short by necessity: the Till case has a tight circle of direct witnesses, and the corpus preserves most of it. The path moves from the family in Money to the Chicago funeral coverage to the NAACP mobilization to the Black Mississippi ministry that drew its early radicalization from the killing.

path.note: Till's first cousin and eyewitness to the kidnapping from the Wright family home in Money, sleeping in the adjacent room when Milam and Bryant arrived after midnight. The last living witness; has testified at Mississippi Freedom Trail markers for decades.

Till's younger cousin who at age 12 shared the bedroom Till was taken from. His father Mose Wright's act of identifying Milam and Bryant in the Sumner courtroom forced the family's relocation to Argo, Illinois.

Booker, Jet's Washington bureau chief, covered Till's open-casket funeral; the visual record that drove the national mobilization. Newson covered Hoxie and Clinton school desegregations and was on the bombed Anniston Freedom Ride bus.
NAACP national-staff field secretary who worked on the 1955 Emmett Till mobilization out of the New York office under Roy Wilkins.
NAACP national-office stenographer who traveled with LDF lawyers on the Emmett Till case and the Groveland Four prosecutions; married Thurgood Marshall in 1955.
Jackson-born Baptist minister whose account threads the Emmett Till murder and the Rev. George Lee murder (also 1955) through the personalist theology King brought from Boston University.
South Carolina SNCC organizer who entered the movement after Till's murder; later programs secretary and Mississippi Freedom Summer Holly Springs project director.

tours.closing: The number of activists who name 1955 as the moment they understood the country differently is the measure of what one open casket did.

tours.title: The legal infrastructure
tours.subtitle: Lawyers who built the case-law foundation.
tours.body:
The civil-rights case law of the twentieth century was litigated by a small network of attorneys: the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund in New York under Thurgood Marshall and his successor Jack Greenberg; the cooperating-counsel firms in the Southern cities — Collins, Douglas, and Elie in New Orleans, Matthew J. Perry's South Carolina practice, Ernest Finney's Sumter office; and the Justice Department Civil Rights Division attorneys under John Doar. The voices in this tour worked on Brown v. Board of Education, the lunch-counter prosecutions of the early 1960s, Title VII employment cases, the Bogalusa litigation that paired with the Deacons for Defense, and the 1971 Swann busing decision.

The path moves from the LDF national office (Carter, Greenberg, Suyat Marshall) to the Southern cooperating counsel (Perry, Finney, Sobol, Hill Jr.) to the Justice Department side (Rosenberg) to the King-circle counsel (Jones).

path.note: Joined Marshall's LDF in 1944 after clerking for William H. Hastie; argued McLaurin v. Oklahoma and helped engineer the June 1950 strategic shift from equalization suits to a direct attack on Plessy v. Ferguson.

LDF attorney from 1949 who co-tried the Topeka case folded into Brown v. Board; succeeded Marshall as director-counsel in 1961. Tenure covers Title VII litigation, the LDF Black-lawyer training program, and Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg in 1971.
LDF stenographer who took minutes at the NAACP conventions through the Brown years and traveled with the lawyers on the Groveland Four and Emmett Till cases. Married Thurgood Marshall in 1955.

South Carolina LDF cooperating counsel and later US District Judge who tried hundreds of segregation cases from the early 1950s. Witnessed Elmore v. Rice, Wrighten v. Board of Trustees, and Briggs v. Elliott in J. Waties Waring's Columbia courtroom.

Sumter attorney who defended Friendship Junior College sit-in students at Rock Hill in 1961 and the 1968 Orangeburg massacre protesters. First Black Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction; eventually Chief Justice.

New Orleans Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee attorney who litigated Title VII cases against Crown Zellerbach on behalf of the Black union in Bogalusa, partnered with Collins, Douglas, and Elie on Duncan v. Perez.

Son of LDF attorney Oliver W. Hill Sr. and an early Richmond public-school desegregator under the city's pupil placement board. Virginia State psychology professor who collaborates with Bob Moses's Algebra Project.

Justice Department Civil Rights Division attorneys under John Doar through the 1960s. Prosecuted voting-rights and church-burning cases; founded Appalachian Research and Defense Fund in Kentucky in 1970.

Attorney and draft speechwriter to King who helped prepare the 'I Have a Dream' speech, served as personal counsel through Birmingham and Albany, and places King's anti-Vietnam turn within an indivisible legacy of racism, militarism, and wealth disparity.

tours.closing: The cases that built the Fourteenth Amendment into a useful instrument were tried by people whose names rarely appear next to the precedents they made.

tours.title: The Black church as movement infrastructure
tours.subtitle: Pastors, deacons, and congregational organizing.
tours.body:
The Black church's role in the movement was institutional, not just spiritual. Congregations supplied the meeting space when no other public room would admit a Black gathering, the fundraising network that paid bail and bus fare, the print shop and switchboard that handled communications, the trusted clergy who could call a town meeting on a day's notice, and the AME Zion / Baptist / Methodist denominational pipelines that linked North Carolina to Alabama to Mississippi. The infrastructure was older than the modern movement — Worth Long's AME Zion network, for example, traces its NAACP-chapter-building work back to his father's generation.

The path moves through the SCLC pastoral core (Walker, Vivian, Lowery, the Abernathy family), into the congregational deacons and members (Anderson, Magee, Bruce), the AME Zion connection (Long), and out to the Western Baptist and Black-press pastors who applied the model outside the South.

path.note: Juanita and Donzaleigh Abernathy on Ralph D. Abernathy's pastorate and the SCLC family-level burden — the household economics of being movement infrastructure.
SCLC chief of staff and Gillfield Baptist (Petersburg) pastor whose 1960 library-integration arrest was the dress rehearsal for Birmingham. Canaan Baptist (Harlem) pastor for 37 years.
SCLC executive staff and Nashville workshop graduate whose ministry illustrates the Lawson-trained generation of clergy on the SCLC staff.
SCLC co-founder and Methodist minister; the long pastoral arc that ran from Montgomery through the SCLC presidency in the 1980s-90s.

Albany Movement first president whose family was rooted in the Criterion Club and Black professional church networks across southwest Georgia; the deacon-physician parallel to the minister-organizer model.
Shreveport SCLC pastor whose Mount Canaan Baptist church built 120 units of low-income housing and a senior tower — the post-1965 redirection of the SCLC pastoral model toward economic development.
Baptist minister and SNCC's first field secretary who built the Albany Movement through Mt. Olive Baptist mass meetings and the rural church network across Baker, Terrell, and Mitchell counties.
AME Zion presiding-elder's son and SNCC field secretary whose testimony documents the church's structural role in NAACP chapter formation across his father's Durham district.
Mount Zion Baptist (Seattle) pastor and Morehouse contemporary of King — the King-circle pastoral model running outside the South.

tours.closing: Without the buildings, the offering plates, and the trustees, the movement would have had nowhere to meet — a sentence to take literally.

tours.title: Western and Southwestern voices
tours.subtitle: The often-overlooked geography outside the Southeast.
tours.body:
The civil-rights canon centers on Mississippi, Alabama, and Georgia. The work in the West and Southwest had its own organizers, its own targets, and its own coalitions — the Chicano Movement (Brown Berets, La Raza Unida, MAPA), the Black population concentrations in Oakland and Los Angeles, the Seattle Black Panther chapter at the city's north end, the Tulsa-Oklahoma City NAACP Youth Council network, the Tongva and other indigenous organizing along the Pacific. These movements coordinated when it suited their purposes (the Watts-era non-aggression pact between Chicanos and Blacks documented in the Arellanes interview) and ran parallel when it did not.

The path moves from Tulsa and Oklahoma City (the Lupers) through Los Angeles (Walter, Mtume, Alexander, Arellanes) to San Diego (Brown, Camarillo) to Seattle (McKinney, the Dixons). It surfaces the Brown Beret-BPP coalition that the standard narrative omits.

path.note: Daughter of Clara Luper who at age eight made the motion to sit-in at Katz Drug Store in Oklahoma City in August 1958 — among the earliest organized lunch-counter sit-ins of the modern movement. Frames the Northern Tour that catalyzed the OKC campaign.
Marilyn's brother and Katz sit-in participant who recalls the Luper living room as the NAACP Youth Council meeting hub and the Charles N. Atkins strategy debates over nonviolent discipline.

Los Angeles teacher and CORE member who tested California's 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act by integrating motels and picketed LA housing tracts for fair employment. Coretta Scott King Award-winning children's-book author.
Tongva elder and founding Brown Berets member in East LA recruited through the La Piranya Coffee House. Documents the Watts-era Chicano-Black non-aggression pact and the Brown Berets free clinic.
Coordinated the Bunchy Carter People's Free Clinic in South Central LA at age 19; later Minister of Finance under Elaine Brown.

San Diego CORE chair and CORE Western Region leader who led 1960s sit-ins and coin-ins against Bank of America and SDG&E. Founded the African American Studies program at San Diego State University in 1971.

San Diego organizer who founded Trabajadores de la Raza and sued San Diego County over employment exclusions affecting Mexican Americans, African Americans, Filipinos, and Asian Americans. Saul Alinsky-trained organizer.
Mount Zion Baptist (Seattle) pastor who hosted King's 1961 Seattle visit. Decades of Pacific Northwest civil-rights organizing from the same generation as King.
Co-founder of the Seattle BPP chapter (1968), the first chapter outside California. Documents the chapter's breakfast-for-school-children program at Madrona Presbyterian.

tours.closing: The Southeastern monopoly on civil-rights memory leaves out half the country — what was happening on the West Coast at the same hours is in this testimony.

tours.title: Children of the movement
tours.subtitle: Second-generation activists and witnesses raised in movement families.
tours.body:
Many of the corpus interviewees were children when they joined the campaigns their parents were already running. Some were the children of murdered organizers (Vernon Dahmer Jr., Grace Hall Miller's children) and some were the children of celebrated leaders (the Luper, Sherrod, and Anderson households). Others were eyewitnesses as adolescents to events that defined the movement (Wheeler Parker Jr. and Simeon Wright at the Till kidnapping, Freeman Hrabowski at the Children's Crusade). The testimony traces the inheritance pattern — what the children took from their parents' work, what they corrected, what they sustained.

The path moves from the lynching survivors (Parker, Wright) through the assassination households (Dahmer Jr., the Miller-Sherrods) to the activist-parent inheritors (the Lupers, the Andersons, the Ladners) and the front-line children (Hrabowski, the St. Augustine Four). It closes on the second-generation BPP brothers in Seattle.

path.note: Till's first cousin, age 16 at the kidnapping, who has carried public testimony of the August 1955 night for over six decades.
Till's cousin, age 12 at the kidnapping, who shared the bedroom Till was taken from. His 2010 Simeon's Story corrects documentary inaccuracies.
Son of Vernon F. Dahmer Sr., murdered by Sam Bowers's White Knights on January 10, 1966. Led the 32-year family campaign that finally convicted Bowers in 1998.
Widow of Hosie Miller (shot in the back, March 1965 in Baker County) and mother of Shirley Sherrod. First Black woman elected to the Baker County Board of Education.
Daughter of Hosie Miller; co-founded New Communities Inc. and won the $12 million Pigford v. Glickman discrimination claim that funded the purchase of Cypress Pond Plantation.
Daughter of Clara Luper who at eight made the motion to sit-in at Katz Drug Store, Oklahoma City, August 1958.
Twelve-year-old jailed for five days during the 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade after Bull Connor spat on him at City Hall. Founded the Meyerhoff Scholars Program at UMBC.

path.entry_subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
path.note: Two of the St. Augustine Four — teenagers who refused to leave the Woolworth lunch counter on July 18, 1963 and were sent to reform school under Dr. Robert B. Hayling's NAACP Youth Council.
Bogalusa Deacons for Defense charter family — Fletcher as Deacons lieutenant under Charles R. Sims; the household's organized defense network and CORE coordination.

path.entry_subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
path.note: Sisters from Palmer's Crossing, Mississippi who organized through SNCC, the Hattiesburg COFO project, and the Atlantic City MFDP challenge — the sibling movement-household pattern.

tours.closing: The inheritance moves both ways — children carrying their parents' work forward, and parents redirected by what their children chose to do.
